none: A purple cloth lays over a surface.

body.creator: g
body.value: Editor Dylan Tichenor has spoken about how Jonny Greenwood composes not to the completed picture but rather as part of the process of crafting the film as a whole: “We give him sets of dailies as we go, but I think it's late in the process when he starts to sort of compose to picture, if he ever does. It's more from talking to Paul, from knowing the script, and from feeling, and I think he does stuff bouncing off Paul that we end up with a whole treasure trove of stuff that, you know, immediately several genius things pop to the top and ‘Whoa, this is great, this is great.’ The kind of theme for the movie, the riddle piano thing is... we came early on in its initial form, and I love that way of working with music, actually. That it doesn't get sort of nailed to picture, that it's a song, that it's a feeling from the composer like a performance from an actor, like words from the writer. You've got this other thing that, you know, sometimes it knows where it wants to go, but other times we're just trying it out. ‘Let's try this here. Let's try that there.’ And it's a great malleable back and forth where notes go to him and cues come back, and we just bat it back and forth” (Oscars, “Academy Conversations: Phantom Thread”).
